title: Nonlocal Optical Response and Surface Susceptibilities: A Systematic Derivation via Spatial Moment Expansion
abstract: We present a systematic theory connecting the nonlocal response kernel of a homogeneous medium to its effective surface susceptibilities for an arbitrary curved interface. Starting from the most general tensorial nonlocal constitutive relation and combining a spatial moment expansion with a distributional thin-layer limit, we show that the full complexity of the interfacial response condenses, at leading order, into a single scalar: the surface susceptibility $χ^s$, equal for the tangential and normal components of the electric field. These quantities provide a constructive generalization of the Feibelman $d$-parameters to interfaces of arbitrary curvature, and the curvature corrections, proportional to the geometric invariants $H$ (mean curvature) and $K$ (Gaussian curvature), are derived explicitly. The formalism is illustrated on a comprehensive set of analytically tractable cases (planar, spherical, cylindrical, and ellipsoidal interfaces) for several kernel choices (Gaussian, Yukawa, tensorial Lorentz). Generalized Maxwell boundary conditions are established and compared with the classical Fresnel results.
summary: This paper presents a systematic theoretical framework to determine how nonlocal optical response kernels translate into effective surface susceptibilities at curved interfaces. By using a spatial moment expansion and thin-layer limit, the authors show that the complex interfacial physics simplifies to a single scalar susceptibility, $\chi_s$. This result provides generalized boundary conditions that account for arbitrary curvature effects, extending classical electromagnetic theory.
main_problem: The paper develops a systematic theory to connect the nonlocal optical response kernel of a homogeneous medium to its effective surface susceptibilities at an arbitrary curved interface.
main_result: The full complexity of the interfacial response condenses, at leading order, into a single scalar surface susceptibility ($\chi_s$), which provides a generalization of the Feibelman d-parameters for arbitrary curvature.
method: The derivation combines a spatial moment expansion with a distributional thin-layer limit applied to the general tensorial nonlocal constitutive relation.
model_or_system: The analysis considers light-matter interaction at an interface between two media, using canonical geometries like planar, spherical, cylindrical, and ellipsoidal interfaces.
key_observables: Surface susceptibility ($\chi_s$), generalized Maxwell boundary conditions, and curvature corrections proportional to mean ($H$) and Gaussian ($K$) curvatures.
important_parameters: Nonlocality range ($\ell$), geometric invariants ($H, K$), and the ratio of nonlocality range to wavelength ($\ell/\lambda$).
main_assumptions: The nonlocal kernel is assumed to be centrosymmetric for the leading-order result, and the analysis relies on the thin-layer limit ($\ell \ll \lambda$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the scattering efficiency ($Q$) and the relative nonlocal correction ($|\Delta Q/Q|$) for different polarizations (TE/TM) across various geometries, showing resonance enhancement.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives the surface response by decomposing the nonlocal polarization into bulk and boundary terms, applying the thin-layer limit, and finally deriving generalized boundary conditions for various curved geometries.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, systematic framework for incorporating nonlocality and geometric curvature effects into classical electrodynamics boundary conditions, which is crucial for modeling plasmonics and metamaterials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
